name: Broadsheet Six-Column Ruled Grid Landing
description: A newspaper front-page rendered faithfully on screen — a visible 6-column CSS grid with 1px ink-black hairline vertical rules running the full viewport height, a blackletter masthead with a thin-rule dateline and edition number underneath, a lead story spanning 4 columns with a 60pt slab-serif headline set in tight leading over a full-bleed editorial photograph, a 2-column sidebar of secondary stories stacked with bold kickers and 14px body text, pull quotes set in 36px italic serif with a 3px vertical rule on the left edge, 'continued on A3' style cross-reference links in small caps at story truncation points, and a classified-ad footer rendered as a dense 6-column micro-grid of 10px monospaced listings separated by dotted rules — broadsheet authority where the grid IS the design, not a container for it.

vibes.mood: Feels like unfolding the front page of a broadsheet that's been printed continuously since 1847 — ink so fresh it smudges, columns so precise they imply a compositing room somewhere in the basement, and a classified section so dense you need a magnifying glass. The vertical rules aren't decoration; they're load-bearing walls between stories that refuse to bleed into each other.
influences: financial times front page print layout

meta.audience: Readers who still fold newspapers into thirds on the subway and judge publications by their masthead — those who equate column density with editorial seriousness and find whitespace suspicious
